questions.questionText: In myopia (near-sightedness), the corrective lens used is:
questions.optionB: Concave (minus) lens (diverging lens — moves the focal point posteriorly to fall ON the retina instead of in front of it)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Myopia = long eyeball → image in front of retina → concave (minus) lens to diverge rays. Why correct: Myopia: axial length too long or refractive power too high → parallel rays focus in front of retina. Correction: concave (diverging/minus) lens → shifts focal point back to retina. Why A wrong: Convex (plus) lens → for hypermetropia (farsightedness — image behind retina → converge rays forward). Why C wrong: Cylindrical lens → for astigmatism (different curvatures in different meridians). Why D wrong: Prism → for squint/heterophoria (deviates light). Clinical relevance: High myopia (>-6D): risk of retinal detachment, glaucoma, macular degeneration. LASIK/PRK: excimer laser ablation to flatten cornea (reduce refractive power). Exam trap: Myopia = Minus = concave; Hypermetropia = plus = convex. Simple mnemonic but frequently confused. Memory aid: 'Myopia = Minus lens (concave) = light too Much focused.' Reference: Khurana – Optics.
questions.conceptTag: Myopia concave minus lens hypermetropia convex
